title: Decontrol of Crude Oil and Refined Petroleum Products

flags.justification: Exempts all crude oil and refined petroleum products from Emergency Petroleum Allocation Act price and allocation controls (Sec.1), notwithstanding the delegations to the Secretary of Energy in EO11790 (this stream, 38.89%) as amended by EO12038 (this stream, 12.5%).

Sec.5 explicitly states 'advance notice of and public procedure on the decontrol provided by this Order would be likely to cause actions that could lead to economic distortions and dislocations, and would therefore be contrary to the public interest' - an explicit acknowledgment that the normal public-input process is being bypassed.

Bundles complete, immediate decontrol (Sec.1) with a set of selectively-continued transitional programs each carrying its own distinct termination date (State middle-distillate set-aside, mass-transit allocation, Buy-Sell lists, Canadian Allocation Program - Sec.2(b)-(e)).

Sec.4 grants the Secretary of Energy open-ended catch-all authority to 'take such other actions as he deems necessary to ensure that the purposes of this Order are effectuated,' with no defined boundary.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
The selectively-continued transitional provisions (Sec.2(b), (c), (e)) each carry a clean, explicit March 31, 1981 termination date.

Immediately and unilaterally decontrols the entire U.S. crude oil and refined petroleum products market - price and allocation controls in place since the 1973 oil embargo - with explicit, deliberate bypass of notice-and-comment rulemaking (Sec.5), the single most economically consequential deregulatory action taken by executive order in this corpus to date.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

This is the long-flagged completion of the crude-oil decontrol trajectory this stream tracked across the Carter administration (EO12153, EO12186, EO12189, this stream, Batch 6 Chunks 8-9) - Reagan's second Executive Order in office, ending nearly a decade of federal oil price and allocation controls in a single stroke, with major, well-documented macroeconomic consequences through the 1980s.

Exempts all crude oil and refined petroleum products from Emergency Petroleum Allocation Act price and allocation controls (Sec.1), notwithstanding the delegations to the Secretary of Energy in EO11790 (this stream, 38.89%) as amended by EO12038 (this stream, 12.5%).
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: Fulfills the cross-batch cross-reference flagged at the close of Batch 06 (Carter) — this is EO12287, the exact order this stream anticipated would complete full crude-oil price decontrol. Unlike the standing-authority concentration findings elsewhere in this corpus, this order concentrates no ongoing power (it extinguishes existing regulatory controls rather than creating new ones); its structural risk instead lies entirely in the unilateral, no-public-process manner of that extinguishment (Flag 7 CRITICAL) rather than in what remains standing afterward.

title: Suspension of Litigation Against Iran

flags.justification: Suspends all U.S.-national claims against Iran pending in U.S. courts and redirects them to the newly-created Iran-United States Claims Tribunal (Sec.1), a sweeping across-the-board override of ordinary case-by-case judicial process.

Genuine structural safeguards are built in: Sec.2 preserves suspended actions from dismissal for want of prosecution; Sec.3/4 provide automatic, self-executing termination of the suspension upon specific Tribunal determinations (lack of jurisdiction, merits ruling, or full payment of an award); and the order's final sentence requires transmittal of copies to Congress.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Suspends litigants' ability to pursue their claims in U.S. courts, but - critically distinct from EO12283's flat claims-bar (this stream, Batch 6 Chunk 12, 75.0%) - provides a genuine alternative forum (the Claims Tribunal) rather than extinguishing the underlying claims outright, so this is scored PRESENT rather than CRITICAL.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Formally establishes U.S. implementation of the Iran-United States Claims Tribunal, an international arbitral institution that went on to adjudicate billions of dollars in claims over subsequent decades - one of the most consequential and long-lived international legal mechanisms to emerge from any order in this corpus.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: The single most significant continuity-of-government finding in this stream to date: Sec.8 explicitly 'ratifies' EO12276 through EO12285 (this stream, Batch 6 Chunk 12, several scoring 75.0% - this stream's all-time high) - the incoming Reagan Administration formally affirming and continuing the emergency actions its predecessor took in its final hours, rather than repudiating them. A clean, well-documented instance of an opposing-party successor honoring outgoing emergency executive action for reasons of international commitment and rule-of-law continuity.

The order explicitly excludes these newly-converted positions from Section 2302 of Title 5 (the Prohibited Personnel Practices protections), removing a real accountability safeguard against politically-motivated personnel actions for the affected positions.

title: Interdiction of Illegal Aliens

flags.justification: Directs the Coast Guard to stop, board, and return vessels carrying undocumented migrants on the high seas, concentrating broad, largely unreviewed interdiction and return authority in the executive branch with no external check on individual interdiction decisions.

While Sec.2(c)(3) includes a non-refoulement safeguard ('no person who is a refugee will be returned without his consent'), the order specifies no formal screening procedure, standard of proof, or documentation requirement for determining refugee status or obtaining 'consent' before return at sea - a real, later well-documented gap in this program's accountability architecture.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Sec.2(c)(1) and (3) key the boarding and return decisions to open-ended 'reason to believe' standards applied by Coast Guard personnel at sea, for interdiction decisions with life-or-liberty stakes.

Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Established the modern U.S. maritime migrant-interdiction program, later central to major litigation (including the Supreme Court's Sale v. Haitian Centers Council, 1993) and to U.S. immigration and refugee policy for decades afterward - among the most legally and morally consequential orders in this corpus.

title: United States Intelligence Activities

flags.justification: Vests the Director of Central Intelligence with broad, government-wide budget, tasking, and priority-setting authority over the entire National Foreign Intelligence Program (Sec.1.5) and grants the CIA exclusive authority (absent a Presidential determination otherwise) to conduct 'special activities' - covert action - abroad (Sec.1.8(e)).

A genuinely extensive, carefully-built accountability architecture: explicit statutory Congressional oversight preserved (Sec.3.1), Attorney General approval required for any technique requiring a warrant-equivalent (Sec.2.5), an explicit categorical assassination ban (Sec.2.11), human-subjects research protections tied to HHS guidelines (Sec.2.10), guaranteed Inspector General/General Counsel information access (Sec.1.7(i)), and the companion President's Intelligence Oversight Board established the same day (EO12334, this chunk) to receive reports of suspected illegality.

Bundles the entire Intelligence Community's structure - goals, NSC role, DCI duties, agency-specific responsibilities across at least eight departments/agencies, collection-technique rules, and oversight mechanisms - into a single omnibus charter, an inherent feature of regulating an entire institutional domain in one order.

Repeatedly keys individual-rights-implicating collection and technique decisions to open-textured standards - 'reason to believe,' 'least intrusive collection techniques feasible,' 'significant foreign intelligence' - without further specification.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

No termination date; a permanent standing charter. This order in fact remained the Intelligence Community's core governing charter largely unchanged for over two decades and, as amended, continues to govern intelligence activities decades later as of 2026 - a genuine real-world confirmation of the durability this flag is designed to flag, notwithstanding the order's careful internal design.
Establishes the goals, direction, agency-by-agency duties, and permissible collection techniques for the entire U.S. Intelligence Community, replacing Carter's intelligence charter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

The foundational charter for the entire modern U.S. Intelligence Community - one of the most consequential and enduring orders in this entire corpus, governing intelligence collection, covert action, and civil-liberties protections for national-security activities for over four decades.

Assigns each agency a distinct, largely non-overlapping jurisdictional lane (CIA: foreign HUMINT/covert action; NSA: signals intelligence; DIA: military intelligence; FBI: domestic counterintelligence; State: overt diplomatic collection; Treasury: financial; Energy: nuclear/energy expertise) with explicit mandatory coordination requirements at every jurisdictional boundary (e.g., CIA-FBI coordination on domestic counterintelligence, Sec.1.8(c); DOD-CIA coordination on overseas counterintelligence, Sec.1.11(d)) - a genuinely well-designed jurisdictional architecture that minimizes cannibalization risk through explicit boundary-drawing rather than leaving agencies to compete.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: Revokes EO12036 (Carter, this stream, Batch 6 Chunk 4, 33.33% CRITICAL). EO12333 went on to serve as the enduring foundational U.S. intelligence charter, amended several times in later decades but never wholesale replaced - it remains, in amended form, the core governing framework for U.S. intelligence activities as of 2026, over 44 years after issuance. The same 'well-designed despite enormous stakes' pattern this stream has tracked in other landmark institutional orders (EO12009 DOE 25.0%, EO12286 CERCLA 31.25%, EO12301 PCIE 25.0%, this stream) recurs here, scored somewhat higher than those given the order's real concentration of covert-action authority (Flag 1) and open-textured collection standards (Flag 4) - both genuine, if carefully bounded, structural risk factors inherent to intelligence-community governance that don't arise in the same way for an environmental cleanup program or an inspector-general council.

title: President's Intelligence Oversight Board

flags.justification: Establishes a 3-member President's Intelligence Oversight Board within the White House Office to inform the President of suspected illegal intelligence activities, review agency legality guidelines, and forward suspected violations to the Attorney General.

The Board tasked with catching unlawful intelligence activity is itself accountable only to the President: members 'serve at the pleasure of the President' (removable at will), report exclusively to the President rather than independently to Congress (Sec.3), and no minimum meeting frequency or public reporting requirement is specified - a real, structural 'who watches the watchers' gap for the entity meant to police the legality of the very agencies the President also oversees operationally.

This Board itself persisted in modified form for decades as a recurring element of the executive intelligence-oversight architecture.

Notably, the Board's own accountability structure is thinner than what it is tasked with overseeing, a genuine and worth-tracking asymmetry between the sophistication of EO12333's internal safeguards and the oversight body meant to enforce them.

title: National Security Information

flags.justification: Reclassification of previously declassified and disclosed information is authorized on the originating official's own written determination alone (Sec.1.6(c)), with no independent review required before the reclassification takes effect.

The Information Security Oversight Office's enforcement authority is advisory rather than binding - upon finding a violation, the Director may only 'make a report... so that corrective steps, if appropriate, may be taken' (Sec.5.4(a)) - and agencies may themselves deny ISOO on-site access to records by citing 'exceptional national security risk' (Sec.5.2(b)(4)), with only a non-judicial NSC appeal available to the overseer being denied access.

Bundles original classification standards, derivative classification, declassification/downgrading procedures, safeguarding/access rules, and oversight-office implementation into a single comprehensive charter for the entire national classification system.

Sec.1.1(c) reverses the predecessor order's presumption: where there is 'reasonable doubt about the need to classify,' information is now 'safeguarded as if it were classified' pending determination, rather than defaulting to non-classification; Sec.1.3(a)(10) adds an open-ended catch-all classification category for information 'as determined by the President or by agency heads'; and 'reasonably could be expected to cause damage' (repeated at all three classification levels, Sec.1.1(a)) is a notoriously difficult-to-challenge standard.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
No termination date; a permanent classification-system charter, as is typical and expected for this order type.

Sec.1.4(a) makes setting a specific declassification date merely discretionary ('when it can be determined') rather than the mandatory declassification schedule found in the predecessor order; Sec.3.4(b) narrows mandatory-review rights by exempting presidential papers and White House staff material; and Sec.3.4(f)(1) explicitly authorizes agencies to 'refuse to confirm or deny the existence' of requested information in response to FOIA/Privacy Act/mandatory-review requests - a real curtailment of the ordinary transparency-request mechanism.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

The direct, well-documented, historically significant reversal of Carter's pro-disclosure classification-policy default - among the most consequential 'transparency direction' orders in this entire corpus, governing the volume and duration of government secrecy for decades and drawing sustained criticism from transparency and historical-research communities for its loosened classification and reclassification standards.

Prescribes a uniform three-tier system (Top Secret, Secret, Confidential) for classifying, declassifying, and safeguarding national security information across the entire Executive Branch, administered by the Information Security Oversight Office under National Security Council policy direction.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: Revokes EO12065 (Carter, this stream, Batch 6 Chunk 4, 27.78% CRITICAL) effective August 1, 1982. Roughly double its predecessor's score, driven by a genuine, well-documented, historically significant substantive reversal: Carter's order established a presumption favoring non-classification in doubtful cases and mandatory declassification schedules, both of which this order replaces with discretionary, classification-favoring defaults. This is the single richest diff-aware comparison in this stream's corpus to date given how directly and explicitly the successor order reverses the specific mechanisms (not just the general posture) of its predecessor.

title: Continuation of Export Control Regulations

flags.justification: Declares a national economic emergency under IEEPA to unilaterally continue the entire pre-existing export-control regulatory and licensing apparatus (Title 15 CFR Parts 368-399) in full force after Congress's own governing statute, the Export Administration Act of 1979, was allowed to expire - substituting executive emergency authority for an entire lapsed statutory regime rather than a narrow stopgap measure.

Declares a national economic emergency under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to continue the Export Administration Act's regulatory regime in full force following the Act's statutory expiration, pending Congressional reauthorization.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Declares a national economic emergency under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to continue the Export Administration Act's regulatory regime in full force following the Act's statutory expiration, pending Congressional reauthorization.

Sec.4 states the order 'shall remain in effect until terminated' and explicitly declares 'It is my intention to terminate this Order upon enactment into law of a bill reauthorizing the authorities contained in the Export Administration Act' - framing the emergency as a brief stopgap. In fact, this exact IEEPA-emergency mechanism for sustaining U.S. export controls was renewed by successive Executive Orders throughout the 1980s and, after a brief 1990 lapse, was re-declared essentially unchanged in 1994 and continued via annual renewal for decades more, with the underlying export-control regime not placed on durable, non-emergency statutory footing until the Export Control Reform Act of 2018 - a documented, roughly 35-year 'temporary' emergency, a textbook confirmed instance of this stream's zombie-emergency-trap pattern.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.2 continues all existing export-control rules, licenses, and administrative sanctions 'until amended or revoked by the Secretary of Commerce' with no new independent check introduced on the emergency-continuation decision itself - oversight remains exactly where it was under the lapsed statute, now anchored to a unilateral presidential emergency finding rather than an active congressional mandate.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Governs the entirety of the United States' export-licensing, dual-use-technology-transfer, and foreign-boycott-compliance control regime - a domain of substantial, sustained national-security and economic significance for the following decades.

notes: This chunk's standout finding, and the first confirmed zombie-emergency-trap firing in the Reagan batch: an order explicitly framed as a brief stopgap 'until enactment' of reauthorizing legislation instead became the operative legal foundation for the entire U.S. export-control regime for roughly 35 years, through a 1994 re-declaration and annual renewals, until superseded by permanent statutory authority in 2018 - a direct structural cousin of this stream's tracked EO12170 Iran-emergency renewal chain (Carter batch), now confirmed in a wholly different policy domain (export/technology controls rather than sanctions).

title: Continuation of Export Control Regulations

Re-declares a national economic emergency under IEEPA to continue export control regulations in force following a second expiration of the Export Administration Act's statutory authority, using language nearly identical to EO12444.

This is the clearer, better-evidenced confirmation of this stream's zombie-emergency-trap pattern than EO12444 itself: EO12444 was in fact properly revoked on-schedule by EO12451 (this chunk, above) once Congress acted, but the underlying statutory-lapse problem recurred within roughly three months, prompting this near-verbatim re-declaration. Historically, this declare-lapse-redeclare cycle continued through further short-term reauthorizations for the remainder of the 1980s, and after the Export Administration Act's final reauthorization expired in 1994, an essentially unbroken IEEPA emergency (re-declared that year and renewed annually thereafter) governed U.S. export controls continuously until the Export Control Reform Act of 2018 finally replaced the framework with permanent statutory authority - roughly three and a half decades in which a repeatedly 'temporary' emergency measure was, at the mechanism level, never truly resolved.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Future chunks should watch for further iterations of this declare-lapse-redeclare cycle as they arise in the corpus.

title: Regulatory Planning Process

flags.justification: Requires every Executive agency subject to EO12291 (Federal Regulation, coded by another coder in this dataset at 54.55%) to submit its entire annual plan of significant regulatory actions - including pre-rulemaking activities like comment requests and document development that could 'lead to the commencement of rulemaking' - to OMB for review and compilation into a single centrally-approved 'Administration's Regulatory Program,' with the OMB Director empowered to exempt agencies or activities from review at his own discretion (Sec.1(a)).
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

'Significant regulatory action' and the scope of required agency submissions are defined 'as the Director deems necessary' (Sec.2(a)) and categorized 'in such format as the Director shall specify' (Sec.2(c)), with exemption authority likewise left to the Director's own determination.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.3(c) requires that, absent an emergency or statutory/judicial deadline, 'the agency head shall refrain from taking the proposed regulatory action until the review... is completed' for any action not previously included in its OMB-approved plan - a binding stay on agency rulemaking pending centralized White House sign-off, extending OMB's gatekeeping authority upstream of the rulemaking process itself.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

This order extended centralized OMB/OIRA review from individual rules (EO12291's domain) to the entire annual regulatory planning process itself, becoming - alongside EO12291 - one of the foundational structural pillars of the modern centralized regulatory-review apparatus that (as later modified by EO12866 in 1993) remains the operative framework governing federal rulemaking as of 2026, over four decades later.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: This chunk's standout finding and one of this stream's highest-scoring orders to date: a genuine, still-operative landmark centralization of regulatory authority in a single White House office, extending EO12291's rule-by-rule review into the entire pre-rulemaking planning process itself. Sec.5's explicit disclaimer of any judicially enforceable right against the government (mirroring EO12291's own such disclaimer) leaves affected parties with no legal recourse against the stay mechanism itself.

flags.justification: Declares a national emergency under IEEPA/NEA and unilaterally imposes a comprehensive trade embargo - import ban, export ban, air-carrier ban, and port-entry ban - against Nicaragua, with full IEEPA implementing authority delegated to the Secretary of the Treasury.

Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

The export ban explicitly excludes exports 'destined for the organized democratic resistance' - an extraordinarily vague, undefined, and politically-loaded carve-out with no criteria specified anywhere in the order for who qualifies as the 'organized democratic resistance,' embedded directly within an active national-emergency trade prohibition against a foreign government.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

A historically consequential emergency at the center of the Iran-Contra-era foreign policy controversy, with substantial diplomatic, economic, and (given the surrounding Contra-aid context) covert-action significance; the emergency was in fact terminated in 1990 following Nicaragua's election - roughly five years, not indefinite, distinguishing this from this stream's tracked zombie-emergency-trap chains.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

The 'organized democratic resistance' exemption is simultaneously a notable exemptions-architecture finding: it creates a formal legal channel for U.S. exports to flow to an armed insurgent group precisely while the recognized sovereign government is embargoed - trade legality turns on political/ideological alignment rather than neutral, generally-applicable criteria.

notes: One of this stream's highest-scoring orders: the combination of a sweeping unilateral trade embargo (Flag 1) with an extraordinarily vague, values-laden carve-out favoring an armed insurgent group over the embargoed government itself (Flag 4 CRITICAL, Flag 11) is a genuinely unusual structural feature not seen elsewhere in this corpus - though, unlike the export-control emergency chain (EO12444/EO12470, this stream, Batch 7 Chunks 6-7), this specific emergency was in fact resolved within five years rather than persisting indefinitely.

title: Superfund Implementation

flags.justification: Sec.2(g) gives the EPA Administrator catch-all residual authority over any CERCLA/SARA function not otherwise assigned elsewhere in this Order, in addition to lead roles over NCP revision, cleanup schedules, and trust-fund budget management (Sec.9) - the Administrator becomes the default hub for the entire delegated statutory scheme.

Enforcement, information-gathering, and inspection functions (Secs.4, 6) may only be exercised with the concurrence of the Attorney General, and all litigation is centrally controlled by the Attorney General (Sec.6(a)) - a genuine, textually explicit accountability check running through every enforcement delegation in the Order.

Implements CERCLA/SARA by establishing the National Response Team/Regional Response Team structure and delegating the bulk of Superfund cleanup, enforcement, liability, and trust-fund functions primarily to the EPA Administrator, subject to Attorney General concurrence on enforcement actions and OMB review of NCP revisions.

Sec.2(e)(1) lets the Administrator define the term 'emergency' - the trigger for expedited removal-action authority - 'solely for purposes of this subsection, either by regulation or by a memorandum of understanding,' with no substantive statutory or textual boundary on that definition.

title: Coordination of Economic Policies for Sub-Saharan Africa

title: Federalism

flags.justification: Creates a 'Federalism Assessment' requirement expressly layered onto the EO12291 (other-coder, 54.55%) and EO12498 (this stream, 75.0%) OMB regulatory-review apparatus (Sec.7), with every agency required to designate an implementation official and route Assessments through OMB.

Directs Executive departments and agencies to apply nine 'fundamental federalism principles' and specific preemption-restraint criteria when formulating regulations and legislation with 'federalism implications,' requiring a written Federalism Assessment for OMB submissions and State consultation before preemptive action.

Directs Executive departments and agencies to apply nine 'fundamental federalism principles' and specific preemption-restraint criteria when formulating regulations and legislation with 'federalism implications,' requiring a written Federalism Assessment for OMB submissions and State consultation before preemptive action.

Core operative terms - 'problems of national scope' versus problems 'merely common to the States,' and 'firm and palpable evidence' of Congressional preemptive intent (Sec.4(a)) - are inherently contestable constitutional-interpretation standards with no fixed metric, left to each agency's own designated official to apply in the first instance.

Directs Executive departments and agencies to apply nine 'fundamental federalism principles' and specific preemption-restraint criteria when formulating regulations and legislation with 'federalism implications,' requiring a written Federalism Assessment for OMB submissions and State consultation before preemptive action.

Directs Executive departments and agencies to apply nine 'fundamental federalism principles' and specific preemption-restraint criteria when formulating regulations and legislation with 'federalism implications,' requiring a written Federalism Assessment for OMB submissions and State consultation before preemptive action.

Unlike EO12498's binding stay on rulemaking pending OMB review, this Order only requires a Federalism Assessment to accompany OMB submissions (Sec.6(c)) and State consultation (Sec.4(d)-(e)) - no binding delay mechanism on agency action is specified.

Directs Executive departments and agencies to apply nine 'fundamental federalism principles' and specific preemption-restraint criteria when formulating regulations and legislation with 'federalism implications,' requiring a written Federalism Assessment for OMB submissions and State consultation before preemptive action.

One of the most cited and influential federalism-policy Executive Orders in U.S. history, directly shaping decades of federalism discourse; later superseded by Clinton's EO13132 (1999), but its core 'federalism assessment' concept persisted in some form across subsequent administrations.

Directs Executive departments and agencies to apply nine 'fundamental federalism principles' and specific preemption-restraint criteria when formulating regulations and legislation with 'federalism implications,' requiring a written Federalism Assessment for OMB submissions and State consultation before preemptive action.

notes: Forms a trio with EO12606 (The Family, this chunk, 50.0%) and EO12630 (Takings, this chunk) as OMB-funneled 'regulatory philosophy' directives issued within a seven-month span (Sept 1987-March 1988), each escalating a distinct constitutional/values-based review criterion layered onto the same EO12291/EO12498 apparatus. Sec.8's no-judicial-review clause is the same boilerplate structure used throughout this cluster.

title: Prohibiting Imports from Iran

flags.justification: Prohibits the import of Iranian-origin goods and services into the United States in response to Iranian state-sponsored terrorism and armed attacks on non-belligerent shipping in the Persian Gulf, with Treasury/State implementing regulations and three specific statutory exemptions.

Unlike this stream's IEEPA/National-Emergencies-Act-based country sanctions (Nicaragua EO12513 75.0% CRITICAL, Libya EO12543 75.0% CRITICAL, South Africa EO12532 62.5% CRITICAL, all this stream), this import prohibition is imposed under Section 505 of the International Security and Development Cooperation Act of 1985 rather than a declared national emergency - meaning it carries no annual Presidential redetermination, Congressional-notification renewal touchpoint, or built-in expiration mechanism of any kind.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.2 carves out three specific exemptions (news publications, petroleum products refined from Iranian crude in a third country, and goods already exported from Iran before the effective date), and Sec.4 gives the Secretary of the Treasury broad additional regulatory exemption discretion.

title: Providing for the Restoration of Law and Order in the State of Georgia

flags.justification: Federalizes National Guard units into active federal military service for an indefinite period 'until relieved by appropriate orders' to suppress violence and restore law and order in Atlanta - a direct domestic deployment of military force under the Insurrection Act (10 U.S.C. Chapter 15), the most concentrated form of executive power this stream's structural framework tracks.

The Secretary of Defense unilaterally determines both the scope of force deployment (Sec.2) and the timing of withdrawal (Sec.4), 'in light of' the Attorney General's recommendations rather than any binding external check, Congressional notification, or reporting requirement specified in the Order's own text.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

The mission is concretely bounded: suppress the specific violence described in the same-day Presidential Proclamation No. 5748 and restore order in the 'vicinity of Atlanta, Georgia' - a defined geographic and factual scope tied to a named proclamation.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.4 provides a genuine, if discretionary, termination mechanism: forces withdraw once the Secretary of Defense determines civil authorities can resume full responsibility, informed by the Attorney General's recommendation.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Responds to the November 1987 Cuban-detainee uprising at the Atlanta Federal Penitentiary; a rare, historically significant instance of actual domestic military/National Guard deployment under Insurrection Act authority in the post-Reconstruction era.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: One of very few genuine domestic military-deployment orders in this stream's corpus; the concrete factual/geographic bounding (Sec.1, tied to Proclamation 5748) and the explicit withdrawal-determination mechanism (Sec.4) are real, textually-specific limits distinguishing this from an open-ended emergency declaration, even as the underlying federalization of military force for domestic law enforcement remains this framework's most concentrated power category.

title: Prohibiting Certain Transactions With Respect to Panama

flags.justification: Declares a national emergency and blocks all Government of Panama property in the United States, defining 'Government of Panama' broadly to include its agencies, instrumentalities, and controlled entities (including two named state banks).

Sec.4 explicitly states the measures are intended only 'to extend the effectiveness of actions initiated in cooperation with the Government of Panama and its President, Eric Arturo Delvalle' and are 'not intended to block private Panamanian assets... or to prohibit remittances' to ordinary Panamanian persons - a genuine, textually explicit self-limiting scope.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Uniquely targets two named individuals - Manuel Antonio Noriega and Manuel Solis Palma - rather than a country's government generally, a more personalized sanctions design than this stream's broader Nicaragua/Libya/South Africa emergencies (this stream, 62.5-75.0% CRITICAL); foreshadows the U.S. invasion of Panama in December 1989, twenty months later.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.3 channels blocked Panamanian-government funds into a benefit account at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York 'to be held for the benefit of the Panamanian people' rather than simply freezing them indefinitely, and exempts transactions authorized by the recognized Delvalle government representative.

notes: More individually-targeted and self-limiting in design than this stream's broader country-emergency sanctions (Nicaragua EO12513, Libya EO12543, South Africa EO12532, all this stream, 62.5-75.0% CRITICAL) - the explicit private-asset/remittance carve-out (Sec.4) and Panamanian-people benefit account (Sec.3) are genuine, textually specific limiting features naming individuals rather than embargoing a nation broadly.

title: Assignment of Emergency Preparedness Responsibilities

flags.justification: Assigns sweeping national-security-emergency preparedness responsibilities - including nuclear-attack continuity of government, wartime conscription planning (Part 12, 23), wartime transfer of the FAA to DOD (Part 14), and Treasury planning to protect Presidential succession (Part 15) - across essentially the entire executive branch (29 parts covering every Cabinet department plus FEMA, GSA, NASA, NRC, OPM, Selective Service, TVA, USIA, USPS, VA, and OMB), centralized under National Security Council policy direction (Sec.101(a)) - the single broadest power-distribution architecture in this stream's corpus.

Sec.207 authorizes each department head to redelegate and further sub-redelegate these assigned functions to any organization, officer, or employee with no additional check specified; Sec.102(b)'s explicit statement that the Order 'does not constitute authority to implement the plans prepared pursuant to this Order' is a genuine, textually explicit limiting mechanism, but it applies only to the initial implementation trigger and does not constrain how departments exercise their assigned planning and redelegation authority in the interim.

Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: This stream's new all-time-high score for the Reagan batch, reflecting the genuinely unprecedented sweep of a Cold-War-era nuclear-attack continuity-of-government blueprint spanning the entire executive branch - fundamentally a planning/assignment framework (Sec.102(b) explicitly withholds implementation authority) rather than an operative grant of martial-law-type power, but with essentially no specified checks on how each department exercises its assigned planning and broad redelegation authority in the interim.

title: Federal Emergency Management Agency Assistance in Emergency Preparedness Planning at Commercial Nuclear Power Plants

flags.justification: Authorizes FEMA to draft, evaluate, and treat its own federally-developed radiological emergency preparedness plans 'as though drafted and submitted by a State or local government' (Sec.4(a)) whenever state or local governments decline to participate in nuclear-plant emergency planning - directly substituting federal action for the ordinary requirement of genuine state/local buy-in in NRC licensing decisions.

Sec.5(c)-(d) authorizes FEMA to assume 'command-and-control' functions itself, or delegate them to another federal agency, whenever 'no competent State and local authority is available' - a federal-substitution mechanism whose only specified check is a cost-reimbursement requirement (Sec.2(c)(4)), not independent review.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.2(b) provides genuinely specific operating principles constraining FEMA's discretion - maximum feasible use of licensee resources first, substitution only as a documented last resort after consultation with Governors and local officials, and OMB resolution of funding disputes.

title: President's Commission on Catastrophic Nuclear Accidents

title: Presidential Records

flags.justification: Gives both incumbent and former Presidents an assertable claim of Executive privilege over Presidential records the Archivist intends to disclose under the Presidential Records Act, with the Archivist required to withhold the records pending resolution (Sec.3(d), Sec.4(b)) rather than the Archivist independently balancing disclosure against privilege in the first instance.

Sec.4(b) requires the Archivist, in evaluating a FORMER President's own privilege claim, to 'abide by any instructions given him by the incumbent President' absent a final court order - letting a sitting President control the disclosure of a predecessor's records, with no independent Archivist determination specified as controlling.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

The triggering standard - a 'substantial question of Executive privilege,' defined in Sec.1(g) only as disclosure that 'might impair' national security, law enforcement, or 'the deliberative processes of the Executive branch' - is broad and self-assessed by the reviewing officials (Attorney General and Counsel to the President), with no further textual boundary.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Established the framework governing Presidential Records Act privilege assertions for over a decade; became the subject of major, ongoing controversy when Bush's 2001 EO13233 extended it further (delaying release of Reagan's own records among others) before Obama's 2009 EO13489 substantially rescinded both orders - one of the most consequential and contested presidential-records-transparency frameworks of the modern era.

notes: A fitting closing order for this stream's Reagan batch - literally the framework that would later govern requests to disclose Reagan's own presidential records, and one of the most historically consequential and contested orders in this entire chunk despite its narrow internal-management subject matter.
